AMAT.headline: Revenue dipped YoY in Q1 on weak foundry/logic, but DRAM/HBM mix rose sharply and a $253M BIS settlement hit margins; the demand signal is mixed.

shifts.trend: FY25 Semi Systems +4% YoY → Q1 FY26 revenue $7.0B (-2% YoY) on weaker trailing-edge logic and foundry demand
shifts.note: Memory strength partially offsets but not enough to overcome logic/foundry softness

shifts.trend: DRAM 27% of Semi Systems mix (FY25) → 34% (Q1 FY26) as HBM tech-transition spending accelerates; NAND held at 4%; Foundry/Logic fell to 62% from 69%
shifts.note: HBM capex is the growth engine; foundry mature-node pullback is the drag

shifts.trend: China revenue -16% FY25 (from $10.1B to $8.5B) → China -7% Q1 FY26 ($2.1B); Taiwan +71% FY25 and +46% Q1 YoY replacing China spend
shifts.note: Export controls still biting but rate of decline is moderating; Taiwan leading-edge now dominant

shifts.trend: BIS settlement $253M legal charge in Q1 FY26 for prior-period China shipment compliance violations; operating margin fell 4.3pts to 26.1%
shifts.note: One-time but signals regulatory risk will remain visible in FY26

AMAT.verdict: Thesis intact for HBM/advanced-packaging cycle; the near-term dip is trailing-edge logic/foundry, not AI-related. The BIS settlement is a one-time charge, not a recurring headwind. Watch whether DRAM/HBM mix continues to expand in Q2.

AMD.headline: Data Center growth re-accelerated from +32% to +57% YoY in Q1 and margins expanded, but China access remains tariff-constrained and R&D spend is rising rapidly.

shifts.trend: Data Center +32% FY25 ($16.6B) → +57% Q1 FY26 ($5.8B quarterly, ~$23B annualized rate); total revenue +34% FY25 → +38% Q1
shifts.note: DC re-acceleration after FY25 China export charge headwinds drag

shifts.trend: Gross margin 50% FY25 → 53% Q1 FY26 (+3pts) on richer Data Center segment mix; DC operating margin 22% FY25 → 28% Q1
shifts.note: Mix shift to Data Center is the driver; R&D +39% creates offset

shifts.trend: FY25: $800M Q2 MI308 inventory charge, $360M partial reversal; Q1 FY26: some MI325 China licenses granted but 25% US re-import tariff applies
shifts.note: Access partially restored but burdened; not back to pre-control levels

shifts.trend: Data Center ~48% of FY25 revenue → ~56% of Q1 FY26 revenue; Embedded -3% FY25 but +6% Q1 YoY — recovery beginning
shifts.note: DC concentration rising; Embedded recovery is a secondary positive

AMD.verdict: Thesis intact and strengthening. The DC re-acceleration to +57% and margin expansion to 53% confirm MI350/EPYC traction. China is partially restored but tariff-burdened. The OpenAI 6 GW deal gives multi-year revenue visibility. R&D cost build-out is the main risk to watch.

AMKR.headline: Organic growth re-accelerated to +27.5% in Q1 across all end markets and margins recovered, while capex stepped up 181% QoQ to fund Arizona advanced-packaging ramp.

shifts.trend: FY25 +6.2% YoY ($6.71B) → Q1 2026 +27.5% YoY ($1.68B) — all four end markets positive: Comms +42%, Auto +28%, Computing +19%, Consumer +4%
shifts.note: Broad-based re-acceleration after a modest FY25

shifts.trend: Gross margin 14.0% FY25 → 14.2% Q1 2026 (+2.3pts YoY) on higher factory utilization; Vietnam ramp drag from FY25 partially easing
shifts.note: Recovery is early; Arizona ramp costs will continue to pressure FY26
Arizona construction is the step-change; 1.8M sq ft facility manufacturing H1 2028

shifts.trend: Apple ~30% and Qualcomm ~11% of revenue in both FY25 and Q1 2026; top-10 = 72% across both periods
shifts.note: Concentration risk unchanged; Arizona is effectively a single-customer bet on Apple

AMKR.verdict: Good setup: growth re-accelerating, margins recovering, and the $2.5-3.0B FY26 capex program is well-telegraphed. Customer concentration (Apple 30%) means the thesis tracks the Apple compute roadmap. No new risks surfaced.

AMZN.headline: AWS growth re-accelerated to +28% and Q1 capex alone hit $43.2B (vs full-year $128.3B in 2025); RPO tripled QoQ on $100B+ OpenAI expansion and Amazon's own Anthropic commitment.

shifts.trend: AWS +20% FY25 ($128.7B) → +28% Q1 2026 ($37.6B quarterly); AWS operating margin 35.4% FY25 → 37.7% Q1 2026
shifts.note: Acceleration confirms AI workload adoption; margin expansion despite infra build
Run-rate has materially exceeded mgmt's 'increase' guidance; infra spend has no deceleration signal

shifts.trend: AWS-related RPO $244B at Dec 31, 2025 → $364B at Mar 31, 2026 (+49% in one quarter) on OpenAI $100B expansion + Meta $50B commitment
shifts.note: Contracted but unrecognized revenue growing faster than recognized

shifts.trend: Revenue diversifying: OpenAI $100B expanded deal, $50B Meta commitment, $5B+ Anthropic investment shows hyperscaler-plus-AI customer mix broadening
shifts.note: Still dependent on AWS enterprise growth, but AI relationships reducing single-customer risk

AMZN.verdict: Hyperscaler demand signal is unambiguous: AWS re-acceleration to +28% and Q1 capex of $43.2B both point to an AI infrastructure super-cycle with no end in sight. The $364B RPO is the most forward-looking confirmation of supply tightness in the ecosystem.

ANET.headline: Revenue growth accelerated to +35% in Q1 and deferred revenue surged to $6.20B, but gross margin compressed 220bps on large-customer pricing and memory supply tightness.

shifts.note: AI Ethernet trials converting to committed orders; cloud titan concentration deepening

shifts.trend: Gross margin 64.1% FY25 → 61.9% Q1 FY26 (-220bps) on large-customer mix and memory/silicon supply cost increases
shifts.note: Structural vs. transitory unclear; management flagging supply procurement as active response

shifts.trend: Product deferred revenue $5.37B FY25 → $6.20B Q1 end (+$826M in one quarter); binding contractual backlog $968M new
shifts.note: Acceptance-period contract conversion is the revenue story; RPO $7.7B total

shifts.trend: Americas 79.7% FY25 → 84.5% Q1 FY26; Cloud/AI Titans 48% FY25 stable; top-2 customers 26%/16% FY25
shifts.note: US-based AI build concentration increasing; any hyperscaler CapEx pause would hit disproportionately

shifts.trend: Memory and silicon supply tightening flagged explicitly as margin pressure in Q1; ANET building inventory ahead
shifts.note: First explicit supply-chain language from ANET — new risk vs FY25

ANET.verdict: Growth re-acceleration to +35% confirms AI Ethernet cycle strength, but the 220bps GM compression is the emerging story. Supply tightening + large-customer pricing discounts = structural margin headwind. Thesis intact but watch whether GM recovers in subsequent quarters or compresses further.

AVGO.headline: Semiconductor solutions surged to +52% YoY in Q1 on XPU ramp; customer concentration tightened sharply with top-5 rising to ~50% of revenue and one distributor at 42%.

shifts.trend: Semi solutions +22% FY25 → +52% Q1 FY26; total revenue +24% FY25 → +29% Q1 as XPU demand ramps
shifts.note: Hyperscaler custom ASIC (XPU) deployments are the primary driver

shifts.trend: Top-5 end customers ~40% FY25 → ~50% Q1 FY26; one distributor 32% FY25 → 42% Q1 (effectively one hyperscaler XPU customer)
shifts.note: Concentration now extreme; thesis is a bet on XPU roadmap staying intact at one or two hyperscalers

shifts.trend: Gross margin held at 68% despite semi mix rising from 55% to 65% of revenue — impressive structural margin
shifts.note: Software segment (35% of Q1) is the high-margin anchor; XPU semi has lower GM

shifts.trend: Semi solutions 55% of revenue FY25 → 65% Q1 FY26; infrastructure software fell to 35% as XPU ramp displaces mix
shifts.note: As XPU revenue scales, overall GM could face pressure unless software maintains absolute levels

AVGO.verdict: XPU ramp is undeniable (+52% YoY), but single-customer concentration is now an explicit risk — one distributor at 42% means one hyperscaler effectively drives the quarter. Thesis intact if the hyperscaler custom silicon roadmaps hold; exits if one of the top-2 XPU customers pivots to internal silicon or NVDA.

CAT.headline: Power & Energy growth accelerated to +22% YoY in Q1 and backlog momentum continues, but tariff cost headwinds are significant and a SCOTUS IEEPA ruling blocked $1B in refunds.

shifts.trend: P&E sales +12% FY25 ($32.2B) → +22% Q1 FY26 ($7.0B); Power Generation sub-segment +32% FY25 → strong continued Q1 momentum
shifts.note: Data center prime power demand cited explicitly across both periods

shifts.trend: YE24 $30.0B → YE25 $51.2B (+70%) in P&E; book-to-bill metrics sustained into Q1 FY26
shifts.note: Backlog has more than doubled in 12 months; covers 18+ months of revenue at current rates

shifts.trend: P&E operating margin ~20% in both periods; Q1 FY26 margin pressured slightly by tariff costs and manufacturing mix
shifts.note: Margin holding despite $2.2-2.4B FY26 tariff headwind (partially contracted through)

shifts.trend: FY26 tariff costs guided $2.2-2.4B; SCOTUS Feb 2026 ruled IEEPA tariffs unauthorized but $1.0B refund deemed 'not probable'
shifts.note: Regulatory uncertainty adds to cost pressure; management absorbing tariffs rather than fully passing through

CAT.verdict: Backlog doubled and Power Generation growth is re-accelerating — the data center prime power thesis is tracking well. Tariff headwinds are a real P&L drag, but managed through backlog pricing. Thesis intact.

CDNS.headline: Revenue growth accelerated to +19% in Q1 and backlog grew; China revenue +36% YoY despite BIS overhang, but the 50%-affiliate rule creates an ongoing near-term risk.

shifts.trend: FY25 +14% ($5.30B) → Q1 FY26 +19% ($1.47B); recurring revenue 77% of Q1 mix (down from 82% as hardware up-front grew)
shifts.note: Demand for EDA broadly accelerating as AI chip design complexity increases

shifts.trend: RPO $7.8B at YE25 → $8.0B at Q1 2026-end; share recognized next 12 months grew from 53% to 55%
shifts.note: Faster near-term recognition suggests pull-forward or renewal cadence accelerating

shifts.trend: China revenue +19% FY25 ($680M, 13% of total) → +36% Q1 FY26 ($189M, 13% of mix); BIS rescinded license requirement July 2025
shifts.note: BIS license rescinded July 2025 opened China back up; Q1 growth reflects the recovery

shifts.trend: BIS license rescinded July 2025 (positive) but Sep 2025 50%-affiliate rule now in force; mgmt flags 'limited' near-term impact but monitors closely
shifts.note: Second-order regulatory risk from affiliate rule could constrain China further — watch Q2

CDNS.verdict: Thesis intact — EDA demand is accelerating with AI chip design complexity. China recovery is real (+36%) and backlog is growing. The 50%-affiliate rule is the single new risk that deserves monitoring; if it widens, China upside reverses quickly.

CEG.headline: Calpine closed Jan 7, 2026, transforming CEG into the world's largest private power producer at 55 GW; nuclear AI data-center demand now explicitly the multi-decade growth thesis.

shifts.trend: Q3 2025: Calpine pending; FY25 10-K: closed at ~$22B, adding 23 GW (27 GW pre-divest); combined 55 GW; 2.5M retail accounts including 75% of Fortune 100
shifts.note: Step-change in scale; organic nuclear revenue grows as PPA/ZEC extensions lock in pricing

shifts.trend: Q3 2025: Clinton PPA with Meta signed; FY25: Crane $1B DOE loan guarantee; NY ZEC extended through 2049; 80-year relicensing pipeline activated
shifts.note: Capital light for nuclear relicensing (PTC + DOE loans); heavy for Calpine integration

shifts.trend: OBBBA clean energy PTC 45Y enacted; NY ZEC extended; IRA nuclear PTC (45U) confirmed for fleet; DOE loan guarantee Crane $1B
shifts.note: Regulatory tailwinds stacking in nuclear's favor for the first time in decades

shifts.trend: Adjusted EPS $9.39 FY25 vs $8.67 FY24 (+8%); GAAP net income declined YoY on lower nuclear PTC baseline
shifts.note: Lower nuclear PTC is structural; long-term margin expansion driven by AI PPA pricing and Calpine synergies

CEG.verdict: The Calpine close is the transformative event — CEG is now a 55 GW diversified power producer with nuclear as a premium-priced baseload backbone. AI data-center demand is explicitly the demand driver. The regulatory regime has shifted firmly in nuclear's favor. Thesis intact and strengthening.

CLS.headline: CCS growth accelerated from +42% to +76% YoY in Q1 and HPS (networking switches) stayed at ~42% of total; revolver upsized $1B to fund hyperscaler-scale capacity.

shifts.trend: Total revenue +53% Q1 YoY ($4.05B) vs FY25 total revenue $12.2B at uneven cadence; CCS +76% Q1 vs +42% FY25; Enterprise end-market reversed: -19% FY25 → +101% Q1 on next-gen AI/ML compute ramp
shifts.note: Enterprise inflection confirms the paused hyperscaler compute program resumed

shifts.trend: HPS (networking switches) ~41% of total FY25 → ~42% Q1; CCS ~80% of total both periods; mix holding while revenue scales
shifts.note: HPS share stable as both HPS and non-HPS CCS programs grow together

shifts.trend: Top-3 CCS customers 32%/14%/12% in FY25; top-10 = 79%; Q1 CCS 80% of total revenue vs 70% Q1 2025 — hyperscaler dependence deepening
shifts.note: Revenue increasingly concentrated in 2-3 hyperscaler switch programs

shifts.trend: FY26 capex ~$1B (vs historical 1.5-2.0% of revenue); Q1 revolver upsized from $750M to $1.75B + Term A refinanced to $250M (April 2031 maturity)
shifts.note: Balance sheet being restructured for multi-year hyperscaler capacity commitments

CLS.verdict: Thesis intact and accelerating — CCS re-acceleration to +76% with Enterprise flipping positive confirms multiple hyperscaler demand vectors firing simultaneously. Concentration risk is real but manageable given long-term switch program contracts. Capital structure upgraded for the build-out.

CMI.headline: Power Systems growth accelerated and EBITDA margins expanded sharply to 29.5% in Q1, with data center backlog still stretching 6-8 quarters; truck weakness offsets elsewhere.

shifts.trend: Power Generation +19% FY25 → +28% Q1 FY26; Power Systems total +16% FY25 → +19% Q1; North America and China both cited as demand drivers
shifts.note: Data center demand remains the primary stated driver of Power Generation growth

shifts.trend: Power Systems EBITDA margin 22.7% FY25 → 29.5% Q1 FY26 (+680bps) on operating leverage, lower net tariff costs, favorable pricing
shifts.note: Step-change in margin profile; data center mix enriches ASPs relative to truck

shifts.trend: Data center backlog extends 6-8 quarters in both periods; capacity ramp ongoing; no easing language added
shifts.note: Mgmt visibility is multi-year; backlog duration unchanged sequentially

shifts.trend: Power Systems strong; Engine segment (trucks) EBITDA -$179M YoY in Q1 on lower volumes; Accelera zero-emission business exiting
shifts.note: Truck weakness is structural not cyclical; Accelera exit removes drag

CMI.verdict: Power Systems thesis intact — acceleration plus margin expansion confirms pricing power and operational leverage. The 6-8 quarter backlog is a durable signal. Truck weakness is fully understood and ring-fenced.

COHR.headline: Datacenter & Communications accelerated from +34% to +41% YoY with segment profit +49%; portfolio narrowed further toward AI optics as Munich and aerospace were divested.

shifts.trend: D&C revenue +34% FQ2 ($1.21B) → +41% FQ3 ($1.36B); segment profit +44% FQ2 → +49% FQ3; 9-month D&C revenue $3.66B +34% YoY
shifts.note: Quarter-over-quarter sequential acceleration in both revenue and profit growth rate

shifts.trend: Gross margin +140bps FQ2 (to 37%) → +243bps FQ3 YoY (to 38%); margin expansion compounding as scale increases
shifts.note: Yield improvements and pricing optimization in AI datacenter transceivers are the driver

shifts.trend: FQ2: aerospace/defense divested Sep 2025 (-$115M gain); FQ3: Munich Germany business divested Jan 2026; portfolio increasingly pure AI/datacenter optics
shifts.note: Each divestiture removes Industrial drag and concentrates capital in D&C

shifts.trend: FQ2: Datacenter + DCI + telecom cited; FQ3 adds 'scale across' interconnect as a new demand category alongside DCI and telecom
shifts.note: First naming of inter-datacenter AI scale-across optics as a distinct revenue driver

COHR.verdict: Thesis intact and strengthening. D&C growth is accelerating and margin expansion is compounding. The addition of 'scale across' inter-DC optics as a new named category in FQ3 is a positive signal — it suggests AI datacenter interconnect is broadening beyond intra-rack DCI.

CRM.headline: FY26 RPO grew to $72.4B (+14%) with cRPO outpacing at +16%, signaling near-term demand acceleration; Informatica $9.6B closed to power the agentic data layer.

shifts.trend: Q3 FY26 +9% YoY ($10.26B quarterly) → FY26 +10% ($41.5B) — steady but not accelerating organically
shifts.note: Agentforce/AI consumption is still early; growth rate is consistent but not yet in re-acceleration

shifts.trend: Total RPO $59.5B (Q3-end, Oct 2025) → $72.4B (FY26-end, Jan 2026, +14% YoY); cRPO +16% YoY — cRPO outpacing total RPO suggests near-term demand is building
shifts.note: Informatica contributed $0.4B revenue in FY26; its full-year impact will show in FY27

shifts.trend: Operating margin 19% Q3 FY26 → 20% FY26 as restructuring benefits and AI productivity compound
shifts.note: Mgmt targeting further margin expansion through restructuring and AI/Data Cloud investment efficiency

shifts.trend: FY26 buybacks $12.7B + dividends $1.6B; OCF $15.0B (+15%); Informatica leverage constrains near-term additional M&A
shifts.note: Cash return is robust; Informatica debt paydown will be FY27 priority

CRM.verdict: Steady compounder; no inflection yet from Agentforce consumption pricing. The cRPO outpacing total RPO is the leading indicator to watch — if it sustains, FY27 could inflect. Informatica adds the data layer needed for true agentic AI workflows. Thesis intact but requires cRPO acceleration confirmation.

CRWV.headline: RPO surged from $60.7B to $98.8B in one quarter as new contracts signed; Microsoft concentration fell to 45% from 67% as Meta emerged at 20%; but leverage grew to ~$29B post-quarter.

shifts.trend: FY25 +168% YoY ($5.1B) → Q1 2026 +112% YoY ($2.08B quarterly) — still hyper-growth but rate slowing as base grows
shifts.note: Deceleration is mathematical on the law of large numbers; absolute dollar growth still enormous

shifts.trend: Microsoft 67% of FY25 revenue → 45% of Q1 2026 revenue; Meta emerges as Customer B at 20% of Q1
shifts.note: Diversification is healthy but both customers remain under long-term committed contracts

shifts.trend: RPO $60.7B at Dec 31, 2025 → $98.8B at Mar 31, 2026 (+63% in one quarter); 36% recognized next 24 months
shifts.note: RPO growth rate exceeds revenue growth — contract signings accelerating relative to revenue

shifts.trend: FY25 total debt $21.6B (avg ~10% rate); Q1 2026 post-quarter: $4.0B converts + $2.8B senior notes + $1.0B equity raised = ~$28-29B total debt post
shifts.note: Debt is contractually matched to RPO cash flows but execution/construction risk grows with scale

CRWV.verdict: Thesis intact — RPO growth is extraordinary and customer diversification is improving. The leverage build is a structural feature of the model, not a new risk, but it makes CRWV sensitive to any RPO slip or customer credit event. Microsoft concentration easing to 45% is clearly positive.

CSCO.headline: Networking growth accelerated from +15% to +21% YoY across the two quarters on AI Infrastructure and Campus strength; Security softened -4% on Splunk subscription transition.

shifts.trend: Total revenue +8% Q1 → +10% Q2; Networking +15% Q1 → +21% Q2 (+$1.4B in Q2 from AI Infrastructure, Data Center Switching, Campus, Service Provider Routing)
shifts.note: Q2 Networking print confirms Q1 was not one-off; AI Infrastructure spend persisting

shifts.trend: Networking: +15% Q1 → +21% Q2; Security: Q1 solid → Q2 -4% on Splunk on-prem to cloud shift and prior-gen product declines; trend expected to continue 2H FY26
shifts.note: Two-speed company: AI networking accelerating while security product transitions drag

shifts.trend: Silicon One purchase commitments +10% in Q1; continued buildup in Q2; explicit obsolescence risk flagged in both periods for hyperscaler contracts
shifts.note: Inventory/commitment build is a bet on Silicon One hyperscaler demand staying strong

shifts.trend: Product GM -0.6pts Q1 → +0.2pts Q2 on productivity offset; consolidated GM stable YoY; EPS +31% Q2 on lower tax rate
shifts.note: Margin volatility from product mix is manageable; Silicon One scale drives long-run improvement

CSCO.verdict: AI Infrastructure networking thesis tracking well — +21% in Q2 confirms real demand. Security headwind is understood and bounded. Silicon One commitment buildup is the risk to monitor; any cancellation creates inventory exposure. Thesis intact.

ETN.headline: Electrical Americas backlog surged to $14.5B (+44% YoY, +32% organic) with customer orders +42% organic in Q1; Boyd Thermal pending adds liquid cooling to the portfolio.

shifts.trend: Electrical Americas +16% FY25 → +20% Q1 FY26; Electrical Global backlog +19% FY25 → +73% Q1 YoY
shifts.note: Data center remains the primary demand driver across both electrical segments

shifts.trend: EA backlog $13.2B (+31% organic) YE25 → $14.5B (+44% YoY, +32% organic) Q1 end; total order book reinforcing further out
shifts.note: Organic order +42% Q1 confirms demand not slowing despite high base

shifts.trend: EA operating margin 29.9% FY25 → 25.6% Q1 FY26 (-480bps) from commodity/wage inflation; 210bps volume leverage partially offsets
shifts.note: Structural inflationary cost pressure is the main concern; expected to abate as pricing escalators kick in

shifts.trend: FY25 acquisitions Fibrebond, Resilient, Ultra PCS; Boyd Thermal $9.5B acquisition pending close Q2 2026; adds liquid cooling for chip-to-grid coverage
shifts.note: Boyd Thermal is the strategic move to capture liquid cooling exposure alongside power distribution

ETN.verdict: One of the strongest backlog signals in the portfolio — EA organic orders +42% with book-to-bill >1.2x means visibility for 2-3 quarters of growth. Margin compression is the near-term watch item. Boyd Thermal adds liquid cooling exposure if it closes. Thesis intact and strengthening.

FN.headline: Growth accelerated to +39% in FQ3, with DCI revenue doubling (+90%) as inter-datacenter AI scale-across ramps; HPC line now $106M quarterly and growing.

shifts.note: Acceleration driven by DCI and Telecom, partially offset by flat Datacom (mix shift)

shifts.trend: DCI $142.2M FQ2 → $196.9M FQ3 (+90.4% YoY in FQ3); Telecom $412.2M FQ2 → $431.4M FQ3 (+42.5% YoY); Datacom flat QoQ/YoY as mix shifts to DCI
shifts.note: DCI doubling is the signal: AI inter-datacenter scale-across is driving a new optical demand vector

shifts.trend: HPC line (AI-relevant non-optical): $85.6M FQ2 → $106.7M FQ3; 9-month HPC $207.7M — now a material revenue line, first disclosed FQ2
shifts.note: HPC is emerging as a third AI-adjacent growth vector alongside DCI and Telecom

shifts.trend: Gross margin 12.2% FQ2 → 11.9% FQ3 (-30bps) — slight compression as DCI/Telecom scale with different margin profiles
shifts.note: Chonburi expansion capex and customer-specific assets are investing for future margin leverage

FN.verdict: DCI +90% in FQ3 is the standout — it confirms that AI scale-across inter-datacenter optics is entering the ramp phase, not just a trial. HPC is also growing toward materiality. Thesis intact; FN is a direct play on the optics layer without the laser chip supply constraints that affect LITE.

GEV.headline: Total RPO surged to $163.3B (+32% YoY, +9% QoQ) and Q1 Power EBITDA margin hit 16.3% from 14.7%; heavy-duty gas turbine order cadence holding 110 in 2025 with Q1 accelerating.

shifts.trend: Total RPO $150.2B (+26%) FY25 → $163.3B (+32% YoY, +9% QoQ) Q1 2026; equipment RPO $64.2B → $75.9B (+18% in one quarter)
shifts.note: Equipment RPO outpacing services — indicates new build orders accelerating relative to maintenance

shifts.trend: FY25 Power +9% organic; Q1 Power revenue +13%; Q1 Power EBITDA +57% on favorable pricing and volume; HA-Turbine orders 12 Q1 vs 8 prior Q1
shifts.note: HA-Turbine order intensity accelerating: 8 FY23 → 25 FY24 → 43 FY25 → 12 in Q1 FY26 alone

shifts.trend: Power EBITDA margin 12.5% FY24 → 14.7% FY25 → 16.3% Q1 FY26 — three consecutive periods of expansion
shifts.note: Favorable pricing on new equipment + higher contractual service revenue mix driving margin

shifts.trend: Prolec GE acquisition ($5.3B transformer JV full control) closed Feb 2026; first commercial SMR contract signed; Electrification HVDC and substation demand both cited
shifts.note: Acquisition adds transformer capacity directly relevant to data center power infrastructure

GEV.verdict: Most compelling power infrastructure thesis: RPO +32% YoY, margin expansion for three consecutive periods, and HA-Turbine order cadence accelerating. Prolec GE adds transformer exposure just as AI data centers are driving a transformer shortage. Thesis intact and strengthening.

GNRC.headline: Revenue inflected from -2% in FY25 to +12% in Q1 on data-center-driven C&I +28%; Enercon acquisition and the 'supply-constrained' large-megawatt backup generator market confirm the data center entry is real.

shifts.trend: FY25 -2% ($4.21B) → Q1 FY26 +12% ($1.06B YoY); C&I segment +4.9% FY25 → +28% Q1 on data center customers
shifts.note: Inflection is data-center-driven C&I; residential recovery (+1%) is secondary

shifts.trend: FY25 adjusted EBITDA margin 17.0% vs 18.4% prior year; Q1 FY26 EBITDA +29% on C&I margin 13.0% (vs 11.4%) and residential 25.1% (vs 20.3%)
shifts.note: Margin recovery driven by operational efficiencies and data center pricing power

shifts.trend: FY25 10-K: large-megawatt data center backup generator market cited as 'significantly supply-constrained' creating 'massive incremental opportunity'; Q1: same market commentary with Enercon acquisition to add custom hyperscale power enclosures
shifts.note: Supply constraint language consistent across both periods — structural, not transient

shifts.trend: Reorganized from Domestic/International to Residential/C&I segments in Q1 2026; explicitly signals C&I data center as the strategic core
shifts.note: Segment reorganization is a strategic signal: management is reorienting the company around data center C&I

GNRC.verdict: Revenue inflection from negative to +12% confirms the large-megawatt data center generator thesis is executing. Supply constraints are real and multi-year. Thesis intact; the strategic bet is on C&I accelerating faster than residential recovery fades.

GOOGL.headline: Google Cloud accelerated to +63% YoY in Q1 (3x op income); capex hit $35.7B in Q1 alone; RPO nearly doubled to $467.6B on multi-GW TPU hardware supply agreements newly disclosed.

shifts.trend: Google Cloud +36% FY25 ($58.7B) → +63% Q1 2026 ($20.0B quarterly); Cloud operating income $6.1B FY24 → $13.9B FY25 → $6.6B Q1 2026 alone (33% margin)
shifts.note: Acceleration is sustained and margin-expansive — AI workloads carry better economics

shifts.trend: Capex $52.5B 2024 → $91.4B 2025 (+74%); Q1 2026 alone $35.7B; run-rate implies $140B+ FY26 — mgmt's 'significantly increase' language validated
shifts.note: Multi-GW TPU supply agreements are the driver; also closed Wiz ($29.5B) and Intersect ($5.9B) in Q1

shifts.trend: Revenue backlog (RPO) $242.8B Dec 31, 2025 → $467.6B Mar 31, 2026 (+93% in one quarter); $462.3B is Google Cloud including multi-GW TPU hardware supply deals
shifts.note: Multi-GW TPU hardware supply deals are a new disclosure category — first ever for Google

shifts.trend: Google Cloud op margin went from ~10.4% FY24 to ~23.7% FY25 to 33% Q1 2026 — three consecutive periods of rapid expansion
shifts.note: Cloud op margin expansion is accelerating as revenue scales into fixed infrastructure cost base

GOOGL.verdict: The multi-GW TPU supply agreements disclosed in Q1 are a qualitative breakthrough — Google is now explicitly supplying compute infrastructure to customers, not just running their workloads. Cloud op margin at 33% shows economic strength. Thesis intact and reinforced by the RPO doubling to $467.6B.

HPE.headline: Networking +51% FY25 and +151% Q1 FY26 on Juniper (full-quarter vs 4-month comp); Cloud & AI server segment was -2.7% in Q1 as AI server margins remain 'limited'.

shifts.trend: Total revenue +13.8% FY25 → +18.4% Q1 FY26; Networking +51.1% FY25 ($2.3B Juniper lift) → +151.5% Q1 (full-quarter Juniper); two-quarter YoY compares will normalize once Juniper is fully in the base
shifts.note: Q2 FY26 will be the first clean YoY Juniper comp — watch whether Juniper organics hold

shifts.trend: Networking ARR $3.15B (+63%); Cloud & AI (Server + Storage) -2.7% Q1 on volume/AUP declines despite AI server growth; 'limited margins' on AI servers explicitly flagged
shifts.note: AI server growth is margin-dilutive — GPU pass-through pricing compresses Cloud & AI segment

shifts.trend: Gross margin 35.9% Q1 (+670bps YoY) from Juniper revenue mix; non-GAAP operating margin 12.7% (+280bps)
shifts.note: Juniper software ARR is margin-accretive; synergy realization drives further expansion over FY26-FY28

shifts.trend: Juniper synergy investment $800M over FY26-FY28 for $600M in savings; existing Catalyst $350M cost program targeting FY27 savings
shifts.note: Integration-related spend is the primary capex theme; no major M&A flagged

HPE.verdict: The Juniper integration is the story — Networking ARR +63% and gross margin +670bps confirm the strategic rationale. Cloud & AI server 'limited margins' is a known structural headwind. Clean Juniper compares begin Q2 FY26; that quarter will reveal the organic Networking growth rate. Thesis intact but dependent on Juniper execution.

HUBB.headline: Growth accelerated from +4% in FY25 to +11% in Q1 as datacenter and light industrial strength built; Electrical Solutions organic +10.6% confirms the demand signal is real.

shifts.trend: FY25 +3.8% ($5.84B) → Q1 FY26 +11% ($1.52B YoY); Electrical Solutions organic +7.2% FY25 → +10.6% Q1 FY26
shifts.note: Re-acceleration driven by datacenter strength in Electrical Solutions; Utility Solutions +6.8% Q1 organic adds breadth

shifts.trend: FY25 adjusted operating margin 22.5% (+80bps); Q1 FY26 overall adjusted op margin +110bps
shifts.note: Margin expansion consistent across both periods on price/volume leverage offsetting input cost inflation

shifts.trend: Datacenter and light industrial cited as primary volume growth drivers in both FY25 and Q1 FY26 — same thesis, steady execution
shifts.note: No new verticals or specific customer commentary; thesis is running on organic momentum

HUBB.verdict: Steady execution — HUBB is a reliable infrastructure pick with datacenter exposure through electrical connectors and distribution. Re-acceleration to +11% growth validates the thesis. No new risks surfaced. Lower leverage and higher margin than most peers.

INTC.headline: Supply constraints drove demand to exceed supply in Q1 for the first time; DCAI +22% on ASPs but Intel Foundry loss widened to $2.4B; Intel 14A future increasingly uncertain.

shifts.trend: FY25 10-K: Intel 7 supply constraints hit Q4 2025; Q1 2026: 'Market demand exceeded our available product supply' — first hard-shortage language, expected through H1 2026
shifts.note: Constraint is supply-side (Intel 7 wafer + substrates/memory) not demand-side; could sustain ASP premium

shifts.trend: FY25 DCAI +$794M YoY to $14.3B; Q1 2026 DCAI $5.1B (+22% YoY) on 27% higher server ASPs from premium mix and demand-based pricing
shifts.note: Volume was -5% but ASPs +27% — supply constraint is actually increasing price realization

shifts.trend: Intel Foundry external revenue only $307M FY25 ($174M Q1 2026 after Altera deconsolidated); Foundry operating loss $10.3B FY25 → $2.4B Q1 alone
shifts.note: Foundry remains a massive drag; management may pause Intel 14A if no significant external customer

shifts.trend: CHIPS Act: $6.4B+ funding secured; SoftBank $2.0B and NVIDIA $5.0B strategic investments in FY25; Apollo SCIP equity buyout completing Q2 2026
shifts.note: Government and strategic partner support reduces existential risk of the IDM 2.0 bet

INTC.verdict: A turning point filing pair — Intel Products is being supply-constrained with demand exceeding supply (net positive for ASPs), while Foundry's path to viability depends on the 14A customer win. The NVIDIA $5B strategic investment is the most important signal; failure to secure 14A external customers would be thesis-breaking.

KLAC.headline: Record quarter in Q3 at $3.42B (+11%) with Korea +80% on HBM investment; China fell to 24% of revenue (from 30%) but 9-month China flat — share shift rather than sharp decline.

shifts.trend: Q2 FY26 $3.30B (+7% YoY) → Q3 FY26 $3.42B (+11% YoY); both DRAM/HBM memory and foundry/logic growing
shifts.note: Sequential acceleration on DRAM investment surge; services +16-18% both quarters from installed base

shifts.trend: China -9% YoY in Q2 (30.2% of revenue) → +5% YoY in Q3 (24% of revenue); 9-month China flat at $3.09B as export controls constrain legacy node spend
shifts.note: Revenue share falling but absolute dollars stabilizing; Korea and Taiwan growing faster

shifts.trend: Gross margin 61.4% Q2 → 61.1% Q3 (-30bps); escalating DRAM chip costs for image computers cited as transitory headwind
shifts.note: Margin resilient across both periods; DRAM input cost headwind flagged as manageable and temporary

shifts.trend: Q2: $548M buyback + $250M dividend; Q3: $626M buyback + $249M dividend; $7B added to repurchase authorization; 17th consecutive annual dividend increase to $2.30/share
shifts.note: Accelerating capital return program signals management confidence in cycle durability

KLAC.verdict: Thesis intact — record revenue, accelerating growth, and DRAM/HBM demand driving Korea +80% confirms the memory investment super-cycle. China share is declining but 9M China revenue is flat — manageable. Dividend hike streak and expanding buybacks signal management conviction.

LEU.headline: Backlog grew to $3.9B; Fluor EPC signed for Piketon LEU+HALEU expansion; Oklo JV announced for HALEU deconversion; SWU spot price $200 vs $34 historic low confirms structural shortage.

shifts.trend: FY25 backlog $3.8B → Q1 $3.9B; contingent LEU contracts grew $2.3B → $2.4B (all under definitive agreements pending capacity build)
shifts.note: Contingent backlog converting to real capacity commitments as construction begins

shifts.trend: SWU spot price $200 at Q1 2026 vs $34 historic low in 2018 (+488%); Russia controls 43% of global enrichment capacity; US/allied supply is structurally undersupplied
shifts.note: This is the investment thesis: nuclear fuel security creates durable pricing power for the only licensed HALEU producer

shifts.trend: FY25: $900M DOE HALEU task order; Dec 2025: Fluor EPC contract + Oak Ridge centrifuge manufacturing started; Q1 2026: EPC contract formalized + Oklo JV for HALEU deconversion co-located at Piketon
shifts.note: Multi-front expansion: enrichment capacity + centrifuge manufacturing + deconversion services (JV with Oklo)

shifts.trend: Q1 revenue $76.7M (+5%); Technical Solutions +47% on HALEU Operation Contract; LEU SWU volume -47% offset by +52% in prices — mix shift toward HALEU
shifts.note: Revenue modest but backlog conversion is the multi-year driver; current revenue understates future value

LEU.verdict: Thesis intact — SWU price at $200 (vs $34 trough) is the structurally bullish signal. LEU's HALEU monopoly and Piketon expansion position it as the critical chokepoint in US nuclear fuel supply. The Oklo JV adds another revenue stream. The risk is Congressional funding for HALEU in FY27 DOE budget (flagged as not included).

LITE.headline: Revenue +90% YoY in FQ3 with gross margin expanding 1,540bps to 44.2%; supply allocation language hardened from 'has led to' to 'has required' — LITE is the InP laser chip chokepoint for 1.6T.

shifts.trend: FQ2 revenue $665.5M (+65.5% YoY) → FQ3 $808.4M (+90.1% YoY); 9-month revenue $2.01B +72.4%
shifts.note: Growth rate re-accelerating in FQ3; ramp phase not decelerating

shifts.trend: Gross margin +1,130bps FQ2 (to 36.1%) → +1,540bps FQ3 (to 44.2%); 9M gross margin +1,300bps to 38.8%
shifts.note: Margin re-rating is structural: 200G lane-speed mix lifting ASPs, supply allocation enabling pricing power

shifts.trend: FQ2: 'demand is outpacing current supply which has led to decisions on supply allocation'; FQ3 hardened: 'demand is outpacing current supply which has required us to make decisions on supply allocation'
shifts.note: Language hardened from 'led to' to 'required' — mgmt is signaling more deliberate rationing

shifts.trend: FQ2: 2 customers at 24% and 17% (41% combined); FQ3: 2 customers at 26% and 12% (38% combined); top customer share rose from 24% to 26%
shifts.note: Top customer concentration increasing as LITE's laser chips become essential to hyperscaler 1.6T buildout

LITE.verdict: LITE is the most explicit bottleneck signal in the optics stack — supply allocation hardening + 44% gross margin + 90% revenue growth across two consecutive quarters makes this the highest-conviction bottleneck play. Concentration risk is the only caveat; a single hyperscaler pause would hit disproportionately.

LRCX.headline: Revenue stepped up +9% QoQ in Q3 on DRAM investment surge; memory mix rose from 34% to 39% while foundry slipped — DRAM/HBM is the demand engine, mature foundry is the swing factor.

shifts.trend: Q2 FY26 flat QoQ (+25% YoY for 1H period) → Q3 FY26 +9% QoQ ($5.84B); DRAM customer investments cited as primary driver
shifts.note: DRAM-driven acceleration on HBM tech transitions; installed base customer-support revenue also growing

shifts.trend: Memory 34% of mix Q2 → 39% Q3 (DRAM/HBM ramp); Foundry 59% Q2 → 54% Q3 (mature node pullback); Logic/IDM ~7% stable
shifts.note: Mix shift toward DRAM/HBM is the structural theme; foundry mature-node spending is the drag

shifts.trend: China 35% Q2 → 34% Q3; stable at approximately one-third of revenue across both quarters
shifts.note: China revenue holding despite export controls; legacy node spend supporting the base

shifts.trend: Q3 capital deployment: $1.16B buybacks + $326M dividends + $751M debt paydown; cash dropped $6.20B → $4.77B
shifts.note: Aggressive capital return while cash is strong; management signaling cycle confidence

LRCX.verdict: Thesis intact — DRAM/HBM investment surge is sustaining growth and mix shift toward memory is the right direction. Foundry mature-node softness is the offset but is not structural. China stability at ~34% of revenue is better than worst-case. Q3 +9% QoQ confirms cycle not peaking yet.

META.headline: Revenue re-accelerated to +33% in Q1 and capex guidance raised to $125-145B; non-cancelable commitments hit $237.7B with off-balance-sheet leases of $182.9B not yet commenced.

shifts.trend: FY25 +22% ($201.0B) → Q1 2026 +33% ($56.3B quarterly); ad impressions +12% FY25 → +19% Q1; price per ad +9% FY25 → +12% Q1
shifts.note: Re-acceleration driven by AI-powered ad targeting improvements; both volume and price contributing

shifts.trend: FY25 capex $72.2B; FY26 guided $115-135B at year-end → raised to $125-145B after Q1; Q1 alone $19.8B
shifts.note: Guidance raise after first quarter signals higher-than-expected infra demand from AI workloads

shifts.trend: FY25 10-K: $29.9B senior notes issued; Q1: non-cancelable commitments $237.7B + off-balance-sheet leases $182.9B not yet commenced + $24B multi-year infra contracts added April 2026
shifts.note: Total infrastructure obligation approaching $500B — scale of the AI infrastructure bet is unprecedented

shifts.trend: Q1 operating income $22.9B on $56.3B revenue (41% margin); Reality Labs loss -$4.03B Q1 consistent with FY25 pace
shifts.note: Core Family of Apps operating margin holding strong despite capex surge; RL loss not escalating

META.verdict: One of the most bullish hyperscaler prints: +33% revenue acceleration, capex guidance raised $10B after a single quarter, and $237.7B in non-cancelable commitments signal multi-year AI infrastructure demand. For the inference stack, META is among the most credible demand anchors.

MOD.headline: Climate Solutions accelerated from +24% to +51% YoY; Modine announced spin of Performance Tech into Gentherm (RMT), reshaping into a pure-play data center cooling company.

shifts.trend: Climate Solutions +24% Q2 FY26 (DC +$67.4M) → +51% Q3 FY26 (DC +$130.0M); 9-month DC sales +$221.7M YoY
shifts.note: Data center product growth more than doubled sequentially from Q2 to Q3

shifts.trend: Q2 FY26: announced $100M capex for data center expansion; Q3 FY26: announced RMT to spin Performance Technologies into Gentherm — MOD retains Climate Solutions as pure-play data center + HVAC
shifts.note: Strategic transformation: from diversified thermal solutions to pure-play data center cooling compounder

shifts.trend: Climate Solutions operating margin 17.6% → 13.7% Q2 (ramp inefficiencies) → 15.3% Q3 (partial recovery but still below prior); capacity expansion creating temporary margin headwind both quarters
shifts.note: Margin should normalize post-ramp; both periods show the same constraint

shifts.trend: Q2: $100M capex announced for US DC manufacturing expansion; Q3: $210M cash at RMT close + incremental DC manufacturing lease signed + transaction costs $30-40M expected
shifts.note: Capital deployment accelerating for the pure-play DC cooling strategy

MOD.verdict: Data center cooling thesis confirmed by 51% Climate Solutions growth and the strategic decision to spin Performance Tech — management is betting the company on data center cooling. Margin compression is the investment period cost; should improve as capacity ramps to full utilization. Thesis intact.

MRVL.headline: Full-year Data Center +46% to $6.1B (74% of mix); Celestial AI and XConn closed to add photonic fabric and CXL/PCIe switching; custom ASIC roadmap now extends to 2nm/1.4nm.

shifts.trend: Q3 FY26 +37% YoY ($2.07B quarterly) → FY26 +42% YoY ($8.2B); Data Center +38% Q3 → +46% full year as all four custom ASIC platforms ramp
shifts.note: Growth rate accelerated full year vs Q3 partial read

shifts.trend: Data Center 73% of Q3 revenue → 74% of FY26; Communications consolidated into single segment; Data Center is now the only standalone disclosed segment
shifts.note: Segment consolidation signals management is presenting this as a focused Data Center story

shifts.trend: Direct Customer A 13% Q3 → 14% FY26; Distributor A 34% Q3 → 37% FY26 — one hyperscaler effectively drives ~37% of revenue
shifts.note: High concentration to single hyperscaler XPU customer is structural; thesis depends on roadmap continuity

shifts.trend: Q3 announcement: Celestial AI up to $5.5B → FY26 close: ~$1.3B cash + 24.5M shares; XConn $280M closed Feb 2026; silicon photonics/CPO/photonic fabric added to custom ASIC platform
shifts.note: Acquisitions extend MRVL's custom silicon into the optics layer — direct threat/complement to LITE/COHR

MRVL.verdict: Thesis intact — custom AI ASIC growth is accelerating and the Celestial AI + XConn acquisitions extend the moat into photonic fabric and CXL/PCIe switching. Concentration to one hyperscaler distributor at 37% is the key risk. The 2nm roadmap extension signals MRVL is committed to multi-generation AI ASIC development.

MSFT.headline: Azure growth re-accelerated to +40% in Q3 from +39% in Q2; capex kept rising with 9M total $80.1B (vs $40.9B prior year); RPO barely changed QoQ at $627B suggesting consumption pace matches new signings.

shifts.trend: Azure +39% Q2 → +40% Q3; Microsoft Cloud +26% Q2 → +29% Q3; total revenue +17% Q2 → +18% Q3
shifts.note: Sequential acceleration is the key signal; Azure above 40% for the first time this cycle

shifts.trend: H1 capex $49.3B; Q3 alone $30.9B; 9M total $80.1B vs $40.9B prior year period (+96%); no deceleration signal
shifts.note: Q3 capex alone is as large as full H1 — the acceleration is within the year, not just YoY

shifts.trend: Commercial RPO $625B Q2 → $627B Q3 (+$2B QoQ); RPO barely growing while Azure revenue grows +40% suggests consumption is broadly matching new contract signings
shifts.note: RPO stagnation at $627B after jumping from $290B to $625B last year — RPO phase may be transitioning from signing to consuming

shifts.trend: Q3 operating income +20% on revenue +18% — leverage holding; MS Cloud gross margin under pressure from AI infrastructure but total margins resilient
shifts.note: Operating leverage is positive despite surging AI infra spend; efficiency from AI itself partially offsets

MSFT.verdict: Thesis intact — Azure re-accelerating to +40% with capex at $80.1B (9M) shows Microsoft is the largest single demand driver in the AI infra stack. The RPO stabilization at $627B is worth monitoring: if Q4 shows further RPO growth, it confirms continued demand; if not, consumption is catching up to prior commitments.

MU.headline: Two-quarter beat-and-raise cadence accelerating — Q2 revenue beat guide by 28%, EPS by 45%; Q3 guide implies further +40% QoQ.

shifts.note: QoQ growth re-accelerated from +21% to +75% and is guided to +40% again
shifts.trend: Revenue $13.6B → $23.9B actual → $33.5B guide (YoY: +57% → +196% → implied +250%+)

shifts.note: Pricing power compounding — AI demand outpaces industry supply
shifts.trend: Non-GAAP GM 56.8% → 74.9% → ~81% guide (≈ +24 pts of expansion in two quarters)

shifts.note: Beat magnitude widening, not narrowing — credibility on the raised Q3 guide is high
shifts.trend: Q2 revenue guided $18.7B → printed $23.9B (+28% beat); Q3 now guided to $33.5B (+40% QoQ)

shifts.note: Same constraint language for two consecutive quarters; tightness is persistent, not transient
shifts.trend: 'Supply allocation decisions may impact certain customers' (Q1) → 'Industry supply allocation decisions continue' (Q2) — no easing language added

shifts.note: HBM hyperscaler reliance increasing — single-customer dependency to monitor
shifts.trend: One customer reached 13% of six-month revenue (Q2 disclosure) — primarily CMBU (HBM/hyperscale)

shifts.note: Singapore HBM advanced-packaging fab broke ground; capacity additions from CY2027
shifts.trend: FY26 capex ~$20B (Q1 guide); Q1 capex $5.39B + Q2 capex $5.0B = $10.4B run-rate H1 — on pace for $20B+

shifts.note: First meaningful dividend hike in years; mgmt signaling cycle durability
shifts.trend: Dividend $0.115/share (Q1) → $0.15/share (Q2, +30%)

MU.verdict: Thesis intact and strengthening — beat-and-raise cadence is widening, gross-margin expansion is compounding, and guidance credibility is high. The only emerging risk is HBM customer concentration (one customer at 13% of revenue). Supply tightness language has not eased — bullish for memory pricing through CY2026.

MXL.headline: Revenue growth accelerated from +30% in FY25 to +43% in Q1 on infrastructure optical/wireless recovery; customer base is broadening with top customer falling from >20% to 13%.

shifts.trend: FY25 +30% ($467.6M) → Q1 2026 +43% ($137.2M); Infrastructure (optical DSP, wireless backhaul) +$34.3M FY25 → +$36.3M Q1
shifts.note: Infrastructure is the consistent growth driver; Broadband recovery added in FY25 is sustaining

shifts.trend: Top customer fell from >20% FY25 to 13% Q1 2026; top-10 from 65% FY25 to 56% Q1 — customer base broadening
shifts.note: Diversification improving as multiple hyperscalers adopt 800G/1.6T optical DSPs

shifts.trend: FY25 gross margin 57% (+3pts on mix and lower intangibles); Q1 2026 volume-led growth (no explicit margin guide, prior year levels imply ~57% range)
shifts.note: Margin improvement trend from FY25 likely sustaining as mix enriches toward higher-speed optical

MXL.verdict: Thesis intact — MXL is the optical DSP play on the 800G→1.6T switch transition. Customer base broadening is positive. +43% YoY in Q1 is strong. The key question is whether the 1.6T upgrade cycle has sufficient scope to sustain this growth rate through 2026. No red flags.

NOW.headline: Revenue growing consistently at 21-22% YoY with RPO +25-27% and cRPO +23-25%; Now Assist AI agents deeply embedded — consistent compounder with no inflection yet.

shifts.trend: FY25 +21% ($13.28B) → Q1 2026 +22% ($3.77B) — highly consistent, professional services +19% Q1
shifts.note: Predictable SaaS compounder; no inflection from Agentforce-equivalent usage yet visible

shifts.trend: Total RPO $28.2B FY25 → $27.7B Q1 2026 (seasonal step-down); cRPO +25% FY25 → +23% Q1 — near-term contracted revenue consistent
shifts.note: Seasonal Q1 dip in RPO is normal; cRPO growth rate held near +23-25% both periods

shifts.trend: FY25 subscription gross margin stable; Q1 subscription GM slightly pressured by self-hosted mix; overall operating leverage maintained
shifts.note: AI investment is within existing R&D budget; no margin inflection either direction

NOW.verdict: Consistent, predictable compounder. No acceleration from AI agent consumption pricing yet, but RPO breadth and cRPO consistency indicate the base business is healthy. The inflection question is when/whether Agentforce consumption moves the needle on growth; current evidence says steady state, not breakout.

NVDA.headline: DC growth holding ~+66–68% YoY; customer concentration formally disclosed at 4-way >10%; China is now effectively zero, but supply/energy commentary unchanged.

shifts.note: Slight re-acceleration on a full-year basis after Blackwell ramp
shifts.trend: DC YoY +66% (Q3 FY26) → +68% (FY26 full year); Networking +162% on GB200/GB300 NVLink ramp

shifts.note: Hyperscaler dependence newly visible — diligence risk-additive
shifts.trend: Q3 disclosed 4 direct customers each >10% of revenue (22/15/13/11%); 10-K did not previously break this out

shifts.note: China optionality fully impaired across both filings
shifts.trend: Q3 China $3.0B vs $8.1B prior-year Q3; H20 effectively zero by FY26 after $4.5B Q1 inventory charge; Feb 2026 H200 license carries 25% US import tariff
Non-GAAP GM 73.4% (Q3) → 73.5% guide (Q1 FY27); compressed YoY on Blackwell full-system mix but holding within target

shifts.note: Quote nearly identical across the two filings
shifts.trend: Both filings cite data-center / energy / capital availability as the gating constraint on customer deployments — no easing language added

shifts.note: Vendor-financing footprint growing — watch FCF translation
shifts.trend: FY26 10-K disclosed $17.5B in private/infra investments plus $3.5B land/power/shell guarantees to early-stage AI customers; Q3 added $7.5B new DC lease obligations FY26 Q4–FY30

NVDA.verdict: Story still about supply-constrained AI capex. The new disclosures (4-way customer concentration, vendor-financing footprint) are risk-additive but don't break the thesis; China optionality is now fully impaired and should be modeled at zero.

NVT.headline: Systems Protection (enclosures + liquid cooling) +76% YoY in Q1 with margin expanding to 22.7%; data center is explicitly the dominant organic growth driver at ~46 percentage points of organic Systems Protection growth.

shifts.trend: FY25 +30% ($3.89B) → Q1 FY26 +53.5% ($1.24B YoY); Systems Protection +42% FY25 → +76% Q1 (organic +50.1%)
shifts.note: Acceleration is both organic (data center infrastructure) and acquisition-driven (EPG +$137.7M Q1)

shifts.trend: Systems Protection segment margin +1.4pts FY25 (to 20.7%) → expanded further +220bps to 22.7% Q1 FY26
shifts.note: Operating leverage from volume growth compounding; data center mix improving ASPs

shifts.trend: FY25: Avail EPG (enclosures/power distribution) closed May 2025 (+$1.0B); Q1: EPG contributed $121M in Systems Protection; divested Thermal Management $1.6B cash Jan 2025
shifts.note: Portfolio transformation: exited thermal management, acquired power enclosures for data centers — more focused on cooling/power distribution infrastructure

shifts.trend: Both periods: data center cited as primary volume growth driver for Systems Protection; mgmt expects megatrend to continue in 2026 and beyond
shifts.note: Consistent demand characterization; no easing language

NVT.verdict: Compelling acceleration: +76% Systems Protection in Q1 with 50pts of organic from data centers confirms NVT is a direct beneficiary of the liquid cooling ramp. Margin expansion confirms pricing power. The EPG acquisition is clearly accretive and the divestiture of Thermal was right-sizing. Thesis intact and strengthening.

OKLO.headline: Meta 1.2 GW Pike County prepayment agreement signed (first-ever hyperscaler commitment to advanced fission); order book growing beyond Switch 12 GW; first Aurora deployment still 2028.

shifts.trend: Q3 FY25: ~18,100 MWe order book (incl 12 GW Switch, +4,750 MWe new LOIs); FY25 10-K: Meta 1.2 GW Pike County prepayment agreement; JV with LEU on HALEU deconversion announced Q1 2026
shifts.note: Prepayment agreement is qualitatively different from an LOI — Meta is funding site development

shifts.trend: Q3 FY25: raised $968M in FY25; FY25 10-K: $300M Dec 2025 ATM; FY26 capex guided $350-450M; operating cash burn $80-100M guided
shifts.note: Capital runway extends through 2028 deployment target; Meta prepayment provides non-dilutive funding

shifts.trend: Q3: NRC Phase I readiness 'no significant gaps'; FY25: DOE Aurora Nuclear Safety Design Agreement approved Feb 2026; Phase I pre-application assessment completed
shifts.note: Regulatory milestones tracking on schedule; NRC timeline is the critical path

shifts.trend: Pre-revenue: first commercial Aurora deployment target 2028 in both periods; operating cash burn rate increasing as headcount and construction grow
shifts.note: This is a 2028 revenue story; current valuation is entirely on order book and regulatory milestones

OKLO.verdict: Thesis intact as a long-duration call option on advanced fission. The Meta prepayment is the most material positive development — a hyperscaler paying to develop a nuclear power campus de-risks both the demand and financing sides. The 2028 deployment timeline has not slipped. Execution risk remains the primary variable.

ORCL.headline: OCI growth accelerated from +68% to +84% QoQ and 9-month capex hit $39.2B (3x prior year); RPO briefly plateaued at $523-553B after the initial contract surge.

shifts.trend: Cloud infrastructure +68% Q2 → +84% Q3 (constant currency); total revenue +14% Q2 → +22% Q3
shifts.note: OCI growth is re-accelerating as contracted capacity goes live and consumption grows

shifts.trend: H1 capex $20.5B → 9M capex $39.2B (Q3 alone $18.7B); trajectory points to $50B+ FY26; continues 'next few fiscal years'
shifts.note: Oracle's capex run-rate is now competitive with MSFT/GOOGL/META on a relative basis given revenue base

shifts.trend: RPO $523.3B Q2 → $552.6B Q3 (+$29.3B); large relative to prior year ($97-130B) but RPO growth is slowing as consumption catches up
shifts.note: RPO stabilization after a 5x leap is normal; watch whether Q4 adds new signings or holds flat

shifts.trend: Q2: $17.9B senior notes issued; Q3: added $25B more ($10B Sept + $5B Feb senior notes + $5B mandatory convert + ATM); total leverage rising rapidly
shifts.note: Funding the capex ramp with debt; interest expense growing materially — leverage risk if cloud growth disappoints

ORCL.verdict: OCI at +84% is the most aggressive growth rate among established hyperscalers. The RPO of $553B is extraordinary and provides multi-year revenue visibility. The leverage build is the key risk — $42.7B in new notes plus $5B mandatory convert creates significant financial obligation. Thesis intact but levered.

PLTR.headline: Revenue growth accelerated from +53% FY25 to +85% in Q1; commercial +95% outpacing government +76%; operating margin expanded from 20% to 46% as leverage compounds.

shifts.trend: FY25 +53% ($4.5B) → Q1 2026 +85% ($1.63B quarterly); commercial +60% FY25 → +95% Q1; government +53% FY25 → +76% Q1
shifts.note: Both segments re-accelerated; commercial at +95% suggests AIP bootcamp conversions are scaling

shifts.trend: Q1 FY25 operating margin ~20% → Q1 2026 operating margin 46% (+26pts); gross margin 80% Q1 FY25 → 87% Q1 2026
shifts.note: Operating leverage compounding as R&D/S&M scale slower than revenue; high-margin software model

shifts.trend: FY25: government 54% / commercial 46%; Q1 2026: government 53% / commercial 47%; no customer >10% of revenue in Q1 2026 vs prior concentration
shifts.note: Customer diversification improving; government still majority but commercial catching up

shifts.trend: Commercial growing faster (+95% vs +76%); US commercial $580M at +89% YoY Q1; international commercial $194M at +113%
shifts.note: International commercial growth at +113% is new — global AIP adoption beginning

PLTR.verdict: Breakout quarter — +85% revenue with 46% operating margin is exceptional for an enterprise software company. The commercial re-acceleration to +95% suggests AIP is converting rapidly at scale. Government +76% shows the defense/intel side hasn't slowed. This is a rising-growth-rate story which is rare and powerful. Thesis intact and strengthening.

PWR.headline: Backlog grew from $43.98B to $48.47B in Q1 alone (+$4.5B) and RPO +10% QoQ; revenue re-accelerated to +26% YoY as Electric segment data center demand continues.

shifts.trend: FY25 +20.3% ($28.5B) → Q1 FY26 +26.3% ($7.87B YoY); Electric segment the primary driver both periods
shifts.note: Growth re-acceleration confirms data-center-driven utility and critical-path electrical demand is not abating
Backlog adding ~$1.5B/month in Q1 — suggests demand acceleration rather than deceleration

shifts.trend: Q1 operating margin 4.3% vs 3.8%; gross margin 14.1% vs 13.4% — execution improvement
shifts.note: Tight labor market and material costs still present but margin expanding on volume leverage

shifts.trend: Data center cited as primary growth catalyst across Electric and Underground segments in both FY25 10-K and Q1 10-Q
shifts.note: Consistent demand characterization; data center construction cycle shows no signs of decelerating in Quanta's order book

PWR.verdict: Strong execution — $48.47B backlog and +26% revenue growth confirm PWR is a pure-play beneficiary of the data center construction build-out. The backlog adding $4.5B in one quarter is the clearest leading indicator in the portfolio. Thesis intact.

SNOW.headline: FY26 revenue $4.7B (+31%) with RPO growing from $7.9B to $9.8B and customer growth +21%; NRR held at 125% despite slight moderation — steady AI data workload compounder.

shifts.trend: Q3 FY26 +29% YoY (annualized ~$4.6B run-rate) → FY26 +31% ($4.7B full year); product revenue +29% both periods
shifts.note: Consistent 29-31% growth — no acceleration but also no deceleration

shifts.trend: RPO $7.9B Q3 FY26 → $9.8B FY26 year-end (+24%); 46-48% to recognize next 12 months; weighted-avg contract life 2.7 years
shifts.note: Year-end contract signings drove RPO expansion; consumption model makes RPO less predictive than for pure subscription

shifts.trend: Forbes G2000 was ~43% of revenue; 733 customers at $1M+ trailing revenue (vs 576 prior year, +27%); no customer >10%
shifts.note: The $1M+ customer cohort growing faster than total customer count — enterprise penetration deepening

shifts.trend: SBC as % of revenue declining: 41% prior year → 34% FY26; FCF $1.2B positive; operating cash flow $1.2B
shifts.note: SBC dilution abating while FCF turns positive — quality of earnings improving materially

SNOW.verdict: Steady compounder with improving economics. NRR at 125% is strong; FCF positive for first time. The question is whether AI workloads drive NRR re-acceleration — consumption model makes this harder to predict than RPO-based SaaS. Thesis intact for AI data platform exposure.

SNPS.headline: Q1 revenue +66% YoY on Ansys consolidation; backlog stable at $11.3-11.4B; Design IP weak (-6%); Processor IP sold to GlobalFoundries; $3.5B term loan fully repaid in Q1.

shifts.trend: FY25 +15% ($7.05B, $756.6M Ansys contribution from Jul 17 close) → Q1 FY26 +66% ($2.41B, $885.6M Ansys contribution full quarter)
shifts.note: Optically large — first full Ansys quarter vs half-year in FY25; organic Design Automation growth solid; Design IP -6% is the soft spot

shifts.trend: Design Automation +96% Q1 (Ansys driven); Design IP -6% Q1 — muted growth expected all of FY26 due to BIS restrictions, foundry softness, roadmap missteps
shifts.note: Design IP weakness is multi-factor and acknowledged by mgmt; recovery timeline uncertain

shifts.trend: FY25: $13.5B total debt post-Ansys; Q1 FY26: $3.5B term loan fully repaid; $10B Senior Notes + reduced term loan outstanding; buyback suspended until further paydown
shifts.note: Aggressive debt paydown is the primary capital allocation theme; restoring financial flexibility

shifts.trend: FY25 China -22% ex-Ansys on BIS restrictions; Q1 FY26: BIS 50%-affiliate rule flagged as ongoing risk; challenging near-term China environment maintained
shifts.note: China headwind is priced in and not worsening from Q1 perspective

SNPS.verdict: Thesis intact — Ansys is integrating on schedule ($885.6M Q1 contribution) and Simulation/Design Automation is growing healthily. Design IP weakness is real but contained. The $3.5B term loan repayment in Q1 signals management is executing the balance sheet restoration plan on time. Watch Design IP recovery in FY26 H2.

VRT.headline: Americas +53% in Q1 with operating margin expanding to 27.0%; combined order backlog remained above $15B and FY26 capex of $425-525M is reaffirmed for capacity expansion.

shifts.trend: FY25 Americas +41.9% ($6.39B) → Q1 FY26 Americas +53.1% ($1.81B); total revenue +27.7% FY25 → +30.1% Q1 FY26
shifts.note: Americas acceleration driven by AI hyperscaler data center power/cooling infrastructure

shifts.trend: Americas operating margin 26.8% FY25 → 27.0% Q1 FY26; consolidated gross margin 36.3% FY25 → 37.7% Q1 (+140bps QoQ, +400bps YoY)
shifts.note: Americas operating leverage is the margin driver; EMEA headwind persists but Americas dominates mix

shifts.trend: Combined order backlog $15.0B (more than doubled from $7.2B YoY) at FY25 year-end; remains elevated through Q1 2026 as hyperscaler demand sustains
shifts.note: Backlog is the best leading indicator; doubling YoY provides 12-18 months of revenue visibility

shifts.trend: FY25 capex $226.4M → FY26 guided $425-525M (nearly 2x); Q1 capex $114M on pace with guidance; PurgeRite acquisition also added in Q1
shifts.note: Capacity investment is needed to fulfill backlog; capex doubling before revenue doubles is the correct sequencing

VRT.verdict: One of the most consistent and compelling data-points in the AI infrastructure stack: $15B backlog (2x YoY), Americas margin expansion, and capex doubling to meet demand. No surprises — clean execution of a clean thesis. Thesis intact and strengthening.
